在麦肯锡，我学到的课程之一是结构化思考和交流的重要性。我们经常不得不向忙碌的高管们清晰地提出建议。很多时候，我们只有几分钟的时间来传达该建议-然后，如果高管有兴趣进行更深入的研究，我们可以提供更多细节。
 我们在麦肯锡公司使用的工具之一是金字塔原则，这是一种结构化沟通的方法。
  要以结构化的方式与忙碌的高管沟通，您应该首先从高管问题的答案开始，然后列出您的支持论点。这种“自上而下”的结构对我们许多人来说是违反直觉的，尤其是那些具有科学或工程学背景的技术论文撰写人。对于许多人而言，很自然地可以通过首先叙述所有事实，重新叙述已经进行的所有分析或回顾所有支持的观点来得出结论。然后您到达了妙语。
 在麦肯锡，我们深深地体会到“首先从答案开始”。当一位高管问一个问题-“我们该怎么办？” —您将非常清晰，直接地以“您应该做X”开始您的回答。只有这样，在回答问题后，才应提出支持的理由。为什么？
 首先，您想最大限度地利用与观众的时间。高管们很忙。它们的时间永远很短，习惯于快速处理大量信息，并且在感到某人没有切身之处时会变得不耐烦。为了充分利用您与高管的短暂时间，您首先要提出建议。在某些情况下，执行人员可能已经在头脑中断定了您想要他们达成的结论，在这种情况下，她将接受您的建议并继续前进（而您不必深入讨论详细的论据）。
 第二，许多高管经常以“自上而下”的方式思考。他们希望专注于全局（在本例中为“答案”），而不希望被细节所困扰。通过以“首先回答”的格式提供建议，您就可以适应高管的思维模式，并允许他们快速处理您的建议。
 最后，当您处于直接状态时，您会更具说服力。首先回答高管的问题，您听起来会更加自信和自信。您不是在搜索原因或文字，也不听起来像是在动摇。您是在直接回答您提出的问题。 
您的听众-无论是听众还是听众-都会自然地开始归类和总结您的论点和想法，以便记住它们。因此，您也可以帮助他们做到这一点，并使您的总体建议更加有效和令人难忘。
 金字塔原则主张“书面思想应始终在单一思想下形成金字塔”。唯一的想法就是高管问题的答案。在单个思想下，您应该分组并总结支持思想和论点的下一个层次。然后，对于每个支持的构想或论点，将其进一步分解为更多构想或论点，直到形成金字塔。金字塔原则教导说：“金字塔中任何层次上的思想都必须始终归纳在其下面的思想中。”
  碰巧的是，一个小组中神奇的想法是三个（请参阅我的3条规则）。
 当您对支持的论点进行分组和总结时，可以很容易地从单一的想法转到下一个层次的想法，而无需立即进行详尽的阐述。
  最后，您要确保在每个组下收集的想法实际上属于同一组，具有相同的重要性级别，并且遵循某种逻辑结构。有几种不同的方法可以对属于同一组的创意进行逻辑排序：
 时间顺序：如果存在一系列形成因果关系的事件，则应按时间顺序展示想法。
 结构顺序：将单个思想分解为各个部分，确保您已涵盖所有主要的支持思想。 
当与3规则结合使用时，金字塔原理将成为功能非常强大的结构化沟通工具，用于向忙碌的高管提出建议。在麦肯锡任职期间，我以书面和口头交流的方式引用了金字塔原理以成功地激发行动的过程中看到了许多例子。
 金字塔原则不仅对于与高管沟通很有用，而且与希望说服自己的人进行沟通确实有效。作为企业家，该工具可能是您用来与潜在投资者或董事会成员进行沟通的工具。作为组织的领导者，当您提出重要建议时，可以使用金字塔原则与同行或项目利益相关者进行沟通。
 如果您想了解更多信息，请一定要阅读Barbara Minto的原著。我敦促您在编写下一个建议时尝试金字塔原理，将下一个建议推销给投资者，甚至下一次您回答高管的问题时，都请尝试一下。我敢打赌，您会对结果感到惊喜。
note_en:
At McKinsey, one of the lessons I learned was the importance of structured thinking and communication. We often had to crisply present a recommendation to busy executives. Many times, we only had a few minutes to communicate that recommendation — then, if the executive was interested in digging deeper, we could present more details.
 One of the tools that we used at McKinsey was the Pyramid Principle, a methodology for structured communication.
  To communicate in a structured way with a busy executive, you should start with the answer to the executive’s question first, and then list your supporting arguments. This “top-down” structure is counter-intuitive for many of us, especially those with a scientific or engineering background used to writing technical papers. For many people, it’s natural to build up to a conclusion by first reciting all of the facts, recounting all of the analyses that have been done, or reviewing all of the supporting ideas. Then you get to the punch line.
 At McKinsey, “start with the answer first” was drilled into us. When an executive asked a question — “What should we do?” — you were to start your response with, “You should do X,” very crisply and directly. Only then, after you have answered the question, should you present your supporting reasons. Why?
 First, you want to maximize your time with your audience. Executives are busy people. They are perpetually short on time, are used to processing lots of information quickly, and get impatient when they feel like someone isn’t getting to the point. To get the most out of your short time with an executive, you want to make your recommendation first and foremost. In some cases, the executive may already mentally be at the conclusion you want them to reach, in which case she will accept your recommendation and move on (without you having to go into the detailed supporting arguments).
 Second, many executives often think in a “top-down” manner. They want to focus on the big picture—in this case the “answer”— and don’t want to get bogged down by details. By delivering your recommendation in the “answer first” format, you are fitting into the executive’s mental model and allowing them to quickly process your recommendation.
 Finally, you are more persuasive when you are direct. By answering the executive’s question first, you sound more assertive and confident. You’re not searching for reasons or words, and you don’t sound like you’re wavering. You are plainly and directly answering the question that was posed to you.
  Your audience—whether listeners or readers—will naturally begin to group and summarize your arguments and ideas in order to remember them. So you may as well help them do it and make your overall recommendation more effective and memorable.
 The Pyramid Principle advocates that “ideas in writing should always form a pyramid under a single thought.” The single thought is the answer to the executive’s question. Underneath the single thought, you are supposed to group and summarize the next level of supporting ideas and arguments. Then, for each supporting idea or argument, break that further into more ideas or arguments until you have formed a pyramid. The Pyramid Principle teaches that, “Ideas at any level in the pyramid must always be summaries of the ideas grouped below them.”
  It just so happens that the magic number of ideas in a group is three (see my  Rule of 3 post).
 When you group and summarize your supporting arguments, it’s easy to go from the single thought to the next level of ideas without getting too detailed right away.
  Finally, you want to ensure that the ideas you bring together under each group actually belong together, are at the same level of importance, and follow some logical structure. There are a few different ways of logically ordering ideas that belong in the same group:
 Time order: if there is a sequence of events that form a cause-effect relationship, you should present the ideas in time order.
 Structural order: break a singular thought into its parts, ensuring that you have covered all of the major supporting ideas.
  When combined with  the Rule of 3, the Pyramid Principle becomes an extremely powerful structured communication tool for making recommendations to busy executives. I saw many examples during my time at McKinsey when the Pyramid Principle was invoked in written and verbal communication to successfully motivate action.
 The Pyramid Principle is not just valuable for communicating with executives, but really it’s effective to communicate with anyone whom you wish to persuade with argument. As an entrepreneur, the tool could be what you use to communicate with prospective investors or board members. As a leader in an organization, you can use the Pyramid Principle to communicate with peers or project stakeholders when you make an important proposal.
 If you’re interested in learning more, definitely pick up the  original book by Barbara Minto. I would urge you to try out the Pyramid Principle when writing your next proposal, making your next elevator pitch to an investor, or even the next time you respond to an executive’s question. I bet that you’ll be pleasantly surprised by the results.

手机是便捷的设备，可以方便地将我们与亲人联系起来，支付账单，访问信息，并且对我们的一举一动都进行了狡猾的举报。更糟糕的是，即使在最高法院审理后，许多政府机构仍然坚持要求他们有权提取跟踪数据以查看我们的下落。越来越明显的是，如果您的手机放在口袋里，那么您的脚踝上也可能装有GPS信标。如果您想让政府匿名，则将小工具留在家中。
 国会骚乱之后，当局拿出手机记录看谁在场，就说明了这一点。
 ＆＃34;在国会暴动后的几小时和几天里，联邦调查局在某些情况下依靠不需要法院授权的紧急命令，以便迅速确保在犯罪现场被确认的人的实际通信，＆＃34;截击报道本周。 ＆＃34;调查人员还依靠数据转储＆＃39;从该地区的手机信号塔中提供谁在那儿的地图，从而允许他们从电话中追踪呼叫记录，但不能追踪内容。＆＃34;
 人们的电话和他们使用的应用收集的数据（通常由营销公司收集）非常详细。个人对1月6日的事件感到愤怒。向“纽约时报”提供了当天活动参与者的数据，该消息的作者彻底被这些信息所掩盖。
 ＆＃34;虽然数据中没有名称或电话号码，但我们仍然能够将数十台设备连接到其所有者，将匿名位置与出席者的姓名，家庭住址，社交网络和电话号码联系在一起， ＆＃34;查理•沃泽尔（Charlie Warzel）和斯图尔特（Stuart A. Thompson）写道。
 营销数据库已成为政府机构的最爱资源，政府机构购买该信息是作为第四修正案保护措施的最终尝试。从理论上讲，由于数据是“自愿”的，提供给第三方，则无需政府提供任何隐私权。
 ＆＃34;特朗普政府购买了访问一个商业数据库的权限，该数据库可绘制美国数百万部手机的移动情况并将其用于移民和边境执法，＆＃34; 《华尔街日报》去年报道。 ＆＃34;位置数据是从普通的手机应用程序中提取的，包括游戏，天气和电子商务的应用程序，用户已为其授予了登录手机位置的权限。 
联邦调查局还利用了电话位置数据，这导致了法律挑战，直达最高法院。在Carpenter诉美国案（2018年）中，法官指出＆＃34;大多数法院已经认识到个人在其整个身体活动中对隐私具有合理的期望。允许政府访问单元站点记录……违背了这种期望。结果，法院在首席大法官约翰·罗伯茨（John Roberts）的意见中作出裁决，“政府通常需要获得逮捕令才能访问CSLI [小区站点位置信息]”。
 您可能认为，到访问手机的定位信标功能的政府机构为止，一切就此结束，但是您会误会。手机通过两种方式跟踪我们：通过手机与手机发射塔接触时生成的CSLI，以及通过手机上安装的应用收集的GPS数据。 CSLI尚不如GPS位置数据准确，但它的关键是特定的电话号码，而GPS数据则连接到了可能是匿名的移动广告ID。由于它与个人没有直接联系，并且在Carpenter中未得到解决，因此政府机构认为他们仍然可以收集手机GPS数据。
 ＆＃34;我们了解到Carpenter的决定所涉及的历史小区位置信息与Venntel平台上可用的选择加入应用数据不同，＆＃34;国税局（IRS）最近告诉税务总局（TIGTA），以回应有关使用商业数据库（例如Venntel）的查询。
 在IRS涉嫌对犯罪嫌疑人进行无端追踪的报道后，TIGTA查询是由参议员Ron Wyden（美国俄勒冈州）和Elizabeth Elizabeth Warren（美国马里兰州）提出的。就像联邦调查局和移民官员一样，收税人员也喜欢使用第三方位置数据。美国国税局的论点未能说服监察长。
 ＆＃34; Carpenter判决并未直接解决GPS数据的使用问题，但未来的法院可能会采用相同的逻辑来限制对GPS数据的使用，而无须执行逮捕令，＆＃34;最早由《华尔街日报》报道的TIGTA信指出。我们担心的是最高法院驳回了政府在卡彭特（Carpenter）中关于CSLI确实是自愿提供给电话运营商的论点。法院的理由是，鉴于我们社会需要使用电话，电话用户并没有真正自愿同意共享信息。法院可以对营销商出售的GPS数据采用类似的逻辑，尤其是在政府确定翻译字母数字代码以识别手机所有者的方法或具有其他识别手机所有者的方法的情况下。
 正如《纽约时报》的报道所表明的那样，这是一个很容易解决的问题。 ＆＃34;所谓的匿名ID可以与包含相同ID的其他数据库匹配，从而使我们能够在几秒钟内添加真实姓名，地址，电话号码，电子邮件地址和其他有关智能手机所有者的信息，＆＃34;沃泽尔和汤普森指出。
 可以禁用广告ID，从理论上讲，通过应用程序收集的GPS数据很难跟踪手机。但是CSLI数据会在每次ping塔时精确定位电话的位置。该数据现在受制于逮捕令的要求，但它存在-国会大厦暴动后FBI收集了这些数据。 GPS数据将继续不需手令就可获取，直到法院对此事进行评估为止。 
这意味着任何想要不受监控地参加抗议活动，参加秘密会议或无故旅行的人都必须至少抛弃所有与他们的名字和身份相关的手机和其他现代电子设备。用现金购买并且没有社交媒体帐户的刻录机手机可能没问题，但是就目前而言。互联的现代世界带来的便利是高昂的代价：我们的匿名性。

  即使政府被禁止在没有逮捕令的情况下收集GPS信息，他们是否也不能只是从收集信息的服务中购买信息？我看不出您怎么能说政府需要免费购买的手令。当然，当前可能没有私有公司提供该信息以进行出售，但是，如果有该信息的市场（提示，提示），就很容易做到。
 您可以辩称政府需要发出逮捕令，其逻辑与您不能通过雇用“私人”警察来外包违反《第四条修正案》的行为或通过外包检查制度来违反《第一条修正案》的行为相同。当政府购买“免费提供”的信息时，它将卖方变成政府的事实上的代理人，并且至少出于交易目的，受制于与政府相同的限制。

Cell phones are convenient devices, handily connecting us with loved ones, paying bills, accessing information—and treacherously reporting on our every move. Worse, even after the Supreme Court weighed in, many government agencies still insist that they have the right to pull up that tracking data to see our whereabouts. It's increasingly apparent that, if you have your phone in your pocket, you may as well have a GPS beacon strapped to your ankle. If you want anonymity from the government, leave the gadget at home.
 That point was illustrated in the wake of the Capitol riot, when the authorities pulled cell phone records to see who was present.
 "In the hours and days after the Capitol riot, the FBI relied in some cases on emergency orders that do not require court authorization in order to quickly secure actual communications from people who were identified at the crime scene," The Intercept   reported  this week. "Investigators have also relied on data 'dumps' from cellphone towers in the area to provide a map of who was there, allowing them to trace call records — but not content — from the phones."
 The data collected by people's phones and the apps they use, often compiled by marketing firms, is amazingly detailed. An individual "outraged by the events of Jan. 6" supplied data on participants in the day's events to   The New York Times , whose writers were thoroughly creeped out by the information.
 "While there were no names or phone numbers in the data, we were once again able to connect dozens of devices to their owners, tying anonymous locations back to names, home addresses, social networks and phone numbers of people in attendance," Charlie Warzel and Stuart A. Thompson   wrote .
 Marketing databases have become a favorite resource for government agencies, which purchase the information as an attempted end-run around Fourth Amendment protections. The theory has been that, since the data is "voluntarily" provided to a third party there's no privacy from the government required.
 "The Trump administration has bought access to a commercial database that maps the movements of millions of cellphones in America and is using it for immigration and border enforcement,"   The Wall Street Journal  reported   last year . "The location data is drawn from ordinary cellphone apps, including those for games, weather and e-commerce, for which the user has granted permission to log the phone's location."
 The FBI also made use of phone location data, which led to a legal challenge that went all the way to the Supreme Court. In    Carpenter v. United States  (2018) the justices noted that "A majority of the Court has already recognized that individuals have a reasonable expectation of privacy in the whole of their physical movements. Allowing government access to cell-site records … contravenes that expectation." As a result, the court ruled in the opinion written by Chief Justice John Roberts, "the Government will generally need a warrant to access CSLI [cell-site location information]."
 You'd think that would be the end of it as far as government agencies accessing the location beacon features of cell phones go, but you'd be mistaken. Cell phones track us in two ways: through CSLI generated when phones contact cell towers, and through GPS data collected by the apps installed on phones. CSLI isn't yet as accurate as GPS location data, but it's keyed to specific phone numbers, while GPS data is connected to mobile advertising IDs that are, supposedly, anonymous. Since it's not directly connected to individuals and wasn't addressed in   Carpenter , government agencies maintain they can still gather cell phone GPS data.
 "[I]t is our understanding that the   Carpenter  decision concerned historical Cell Site Location Information which is distinct from the opt-in app data available on the Venntel platform," the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) recently told the Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration (TIGTA) in response to a   query  about the use of commercial databases such as Venntel.
 The TIGTA query was prompted by   Sen. Ron Wyden (D-Ore.) and Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) , after reports of the IRS engaging in warrantless tracking of suspects. Tax collectors, just like the FBI and immigration officials, appreciate the use of third-party location data. The IRS argument didn't convince the inspector general.
 "The Carpenter decision did not directly address the use of GPS data, but future courts may apply the same logic to limit the use of GPS data without a warrant," the TIGTA letter,   first reported  by   The Wall Street Journal ,   noted . "Our concern is that the Supreme Court rejected the Government's argument in   Carpenter  that CSLI is truly voluntarily provided to the phone carriers. The Court's rationale was that phone users do not truly voluntarily agree to share the information given the necessity of phones in our society. Courts may apply similar logic to GPS data sold by marketers, particularly if the Government identifies ways to translate the alphanumeric code to identify the phone's owner or has other means of identifying the phone's owner."
 That's a well-placed concern as the report in   The New York Times  demonstrates. "The supposedly anonymous ID could be matched with other databases containing the same ID, allowing us to add real names, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses and other information about smartphone owners in seconds," Warzel and Thompson pointed out.
 Advertising IDs   can be disabled , theoretically making it harder to track a cell phone through app-gathered GPS data. But CSLI data pinpoints a phone's location every it pings a tower. That data is now subject to warrant requirements, but it exists—the FBI gathered it after the Capitol riot. And GPS data will continue to be available without a warrant until the courts weigh in on the issue.
 That means that anybody who wants to participate in a protest unmonitored, engage in clandestine meetings, or travel untracked has to, at a minimum, leave behind all cell phones and other modern electronic devices connected to their names and identities. A burner phone purchased with cash and free of social media accounts is probably fine, but that's as far as it goes. The conveniences of the connected modern world come with a steep price: our anonymity.

  Even if the government were barred from collecting GPS information without a warrant, couldn’t they just purchase that information from a service that collects that information? I don’t see how you can argue that the government needs a warrant for something that’s freely available to purchase. Of course, there may not currently be a private company that offers that information for sale, but if there were a market for that information –  hint, hint – there easily could be.
 You can argue that the government needs a warrant by the same logic that says you can’t outsource your violations of the Fourth Amendment by hiring “private” police or your violations of the First Amendment by outsourcing your censorship. When the government buys information that’s “freely available”, it turns the seller into a de facto agent of the government and, at least for the purposes of that transaction, subject to the same limitations as the government.

note:
在我2016年1月14日为Grab进行的第一次雅加达工作旅行中，多枚恐怖炸弹从我刚到达的GrabBike办公室爆炸了几英里。人们逃离了袭击现场附近的咖啡馆和餐馆。我的新同事们都为之动摇，为安全起见，很高兴寻求帮助。爆炸周围街区的预订请求激增，证实了街头有人试图逃脱的消息。我的同事们记得2002年的巴厘岛爆炸案，并且知道我们应该让人们散布开来。而且我们知道我们的算法会将其像高峰时段需求一样对待并激活激增定价。人们需要撤离，但如果留给自己的设备使用，我们的AI会以更高的价格劝阻他们。
 我们应该在周边地区免费旅行，全体员工（包括首席执行官Anthony Tan）都明确表示了这一目标。我不知道设置了多少应用通知，车辆类型配置，价格设置，促销代码变通办法，驾驶员激励措施，新闻通讯员，但他们全都弄明白了，在少数几个地方就突然陷入了灾难响应状态分钟。而且有效。
 在德克萨斯州，一场严峻的自然灾害发生一周后，我们听到客户被收取正常电价的75倍（平均电价为每千瓦时12分，最高价定为9美元）。按照这个价格，美国家庭平均每月要支付约8,000美元的电费。州政府或联邦政府提出了各种建议，以支付这些费用，以提供“灾难救济”，而不仅仅是取消此规定。或者，下一步可能是集体诉讼。
 这不仅是政府缺乏计划，法规和激励机制的荒唐失败。这是私营部门从自己的无能中获利的co弱尝试。如果不是恶意的话，那就是问责制。
 我在Grab上了解了更多有关浪涌算法的信息，部分原因是价格和折扣与我启动GrabShare的工作密切相关。作为数据科学家和工程师，我们有责任帮助使价格合理的乘客和驾驶员。主题包括非线性激增，地理平滑，每日时段，价格弹性，功能化和机器学习。这些都不是“子弹头”，而是系统中的工具，其中还包括许多人为监督的保障措施。这些保护措施包括基本知识，例如对涨价倍数的硬性限制-这些限制因城市而异，但从某种意义上讲，我们正在谈论的数字是2和3，而不是75或100。关于价格稳定性，我们有明确的规则和担保-如果我们报价10美元的乘车费用，则乘客可获得10美元的乘车费用。如果在此期间由于交通和需求增加且激增幅度更大，驾驶员的价格变得不公平，则系统混乱了，我们需要对其进行修复。在此期间，我们不会将费用（或帐单）转嫁给乘客。
 公平定价不仅适用于一套已知的规则，而且是一种周到细心的勤奋做法。在AI中，这一点变得越来越重要，在AI中，每个人都有责任预见并防止意外后果。一个典型的例子—当然，我们想到了使用目的地作为预测可接受的暴涨价格的功能。这么多次提出这个建议，以至于一个假想的“医院例子”反复提醒我们-我们将永远不会建立一个可以“学习”目的地为医院的人愿意支付更高票价的系统。声称“没有人真正理解AI系统如何做出个人决定”是一个隐藏的谎言：当我们适当地停下来思考并在向机器学习算法中添加新功能时，我们通常可以提出假设以进行相当轻松的测试，包括必须避免的不良后果。
 这些只是AI和机器学习中的一些道德考量，并且只是组织应对危机的一部分。在Grab的雅加达轰炸反应期间，自动定价激增算法刚刚关闭-我们知道这会造成伤害。在那几个小时内，一切照旧不再了，主要是财务，促销和驾驶员操作负责人争先恐后地凑合在一起。办公室从混乱变成了庆祝：高昂的拳头，因为我们听到了有关乘客逃离安全的第一批报道；媒体报道说，我们的竞争对手Go-Jek已加入我们的行列；那天我们都在一起工作，而且还在工作。当然，这激发了我对Grab的承诺是无法估量的。 
与此相反，我们所听到的有关德克萨斯州电力供应商的信息。就在人们最需要生存的力量时，这一激增被允许飙升近100倍。有些人可能会争辩说“这就是市场力量的运作方式”，好像那是正确的结果而不是错误的设计。救护车驾驶员经常处在人们急需的情况下，但是“市场力量”并不符合道德原则，即向受伤人员收取救护车乘车费100倍的费用。可能有些人认为应该使用拍卖而不是医疗风险来为人们优先购买稀有疫苗。我们大多数人不同意。
 因此，剩下的就是组织无助的借口。 “没有人想要这种方式，它只是发生了。”那是垃圾有人有一个数据库密码，可以立即取消这些离谱的账单。有些部门负责人可能会要求采取这种行动并对他们的首席执行官做出回应。能源公司的首席执行官可能在灾难的第一天就下令“我们提供人们赖以生存的基本服务：在安全的情况下，将取消账单，并在以后确定付款”。他们还没有做到这一点。取而代之的是，我们听到政客说他们正在设法解决问题，甚至包括使用disaster灾资金来填补这些公司的腰包。
 醒醒，美国企业。这与“预防社会主义”或“市场力量的神圣性”无关。不要将您的客户当作人质。那是邪恶的，这样做的公司最终将失败。企业不仅是失控算法的强制执行者，所以不要假装我们是。如果有人阅读本文可以对所涉及的任何电力公司实施适当的补救措施：请抓住这次机会。您今天可以成为私营企业的英雄。
note_en:
On my first work trip to Jakarta 14 January 2016 for Grab,  multiple terrorist bombs exploded a coupl e of miles from the GrabBike office where I had just arrived. People were fleeing cafes and restaurants around the attack site. My new colleagues were shaken, glad to be safe, looking to help. There was news of crowds on the streets trying to get away, confirmed by a spike in booking requests from the blocks around the explosion. My colleagues remembered the 2002 Bali bombings, and knew we should get people to spread out. And we knew that our algorithms would treat this like rush-hour demand and activate surge pricing. People needed to be evacuated, but left to its own devices, our AI would have discouraged them with a higher price.
 We should make trips away from the surrounding blocks free.The whole staff (including Anthony Tan, the CEO) were clear on that goal. I don’t know how many app notifications, vehicle type configs, price settings, promo code workarounds, driver incentives, press communications people set up — but they figured it all out, leaping into a sudden disaster response situation in the space of a few minutes.  And it worked.
 In Texas right now, a week into a freezing natural disaster, we’re hearing of customers being charged 75 times the normal rate for electricity (average is about  12c per kWh, the  peak was set at $9). At this price, the  average US household would pay around $8,000 a month for electricity. Instead of just canceling this, there are various proposals for the  state or federal government to provide “disaster relief” by paying such bills. Or maybe the next step is a  class action lawsuit.
 This is not just an absurd failure of government in its lack of planning, regulation, and incentive setting. It’s a cowardly attempt by the private sector to profit from its own ineptitude. If not by malice, then by unaccountability.
 I learned more about surge algorithms at Grab, partly because pricing and discounts were closely related to my work launching  GrabShare. As data scientists and engineers, we had a duty to help make pricing equitable for passengers and drivers. Topics included nonlinear surge, geographic smoothing, time-of-day buckets, price elasticity, featurization, and machine learning. None of these are silver-bullets — they are tools in a system that also includes many human-oversight safeguards. These safeguards include basics, like hard limits on surge price multiples — these vary from city to city, but to give you a sense, we’re talking about numbers like 2 and 3, not 75 or 100. We have clear rules about price stability and guarantees — if we quote a $10 ride, the passenger gets a $10 ride. If that price in the meantime becomes inequitable for the driver because traffic and demand is up and the surge is larger, then the system messed up and we need to fix it. We don’t pass the buck (or the bill) on to the passenger in the meantime.
 Equitable pricing isn’t just applying a known set of rules, it’s a practice of thoughtful and watchful diligence. This is increasingly crucial in AI where it is everyone’s responsibility to anticipate and prevent unintended consequences. A typical example —  of course we had the idea of using destination as a feature for predicting an acceptable surge price. This came up as a suggestion so many times that a hypothetical “hospital example” became a repeated reminder — we would  never build a system that might “learn” that people whose destination is a hospital tend to be willing to pay higher fares. The claim that “nobody really understands how AI systems make individual decisions” would be a lie to hide behind: when we properly stop and think when adding a new feature to a machine learning algorithm, we can normally come up with hypotheses to test quite easily, including bad outcomes that must be prevented.
 Those are just some of the ethical considerations within AI and machine learning and only one part of an organization responding to a crisis. During Grab’s Jakarta bombing response, the automated pricing surge algorithm was just switched off — we  knew that part would do harm. For those few hours, business-as-usual was no more, and it was mainly the finance, promotion, and driver operations leaders scrambling to improvise together. The office went from confusion to celebration: high-fives as we heard the first reports of passengers getting away to safety; press reports that our rival Go-Jek had joined us in the effort; that day we were all working together and  it was working. And of course, the commitment this inspired in me about Grab was incalculable.
 Contrast this with what we have heard about electricity providers in Texas. The surge was allowed to skyrocket up nearly 100-fold, just when people needed power most to survive. Some may argue “that’s how market forces work”, as if that makes the outcome right rather than making the design wrong. Ambulance drivers are often in situations where people need them desperately — but “market forces” don’t make it ethical to charge injured people 100-times the going rate for an ambulance ride. There may be some who argue that an auction rather than medical risk should be used to prioritize people for a scarce vaccine. Most of us disagree.
 So what we’re left with instead is the excuse of organizational helplessness. “Nobody wanted it this way, it just happened.” That’s rubbish. Someone has a password to a database and could go in and cancel those outrageous bills right now. There are heads of department who could demand such action and answer to their CEO. There are CEOs of energy companies who could have ordered on day one of the disaster “We provide an essential service that people need to survive: bills will be cancelled and payment figured out later when it’s safe”. They haven’t done this yet. Instead we’ve heard politicians say they’re trying to figure something out — even including using disaster relief money to line the pockets of these companies.
 Wake up, American businesses. This isn’t about “preventing socialism” or “the sanctity of market forces”. Don’t treat your customers as hostages. That’s evil, and companies that do this will ultimately fail. Businesses are not just lowly enforcers of runaway algorithms, so don’t pretend that we are. If anyone reading this can implement proper remediation steps for any of the power companies involved: please seize this opportunity. You can be a private-sector hero today.
title: 激增定价，人工智能和责任感
title_en: Surge Pricing, Artificial Intelligence, and Responsibility

note:
我知道，摆脱汽车是您可以为气候做的最好的事情之一，而且您永远也不会这样做。这是一个面向汽车的国家，也是一个面向汽车的时代。但在2019年，普通人开车去上班，购物和休闲的私家车和轻型卡车约占美国化石燃料能源使用量的15％。电动汽车受到了很大的压力，但用于运输的能源不到1％来自电力。在美国，个人运输是造成碳排放的主要因素。这也是最难放弃的。
   但是，将汽油汽车换成电动汽车（或公共汽车或火车）并不是普通市民为减少化石燃料做出贡献的唯一方法。脱碳有两个支柱：首先，从不排放碳的能源中发电，例如风能，太阳能和地热能等可再生能源，而不是化石燃料。这需要立法和法规上的改变。其次，用电尽可能延长您的个人生活。
 那是您和我这样的普通人可以贡献力量的地方。美国至少有7％的化石燃料能源被用于相当平庸的东西：住宅空间和热水。换句话说，在美国进行相对较小，更便宜且容易的家庭供暖更换，可以减少化石燃料的使用，几乎相当于减少所有私人车辆的一半。如果您想为地球带来最直接的好处，请使用新的电器更换老化的煤气炉。
  Y在很多方面。锅炉将水加热并通过散热器加热房间。熔炉将热量传递给空气，然后通过通风孔推入生活空间。
  大多数美国家庭通过燃烧化石燃料来运行这些设备。根据您的地理位置和房屋及其系统的年龄，这些燃料可能包括馏出燃料油（大部分仍在东北地区使用），丙烷（在农村地区常见）或天然气（在其他地方都常见）。其中每一种在燃烧时都会将二氧化碳释放到大气中。
 但是，锅炉和熔炉并不是您唯一的选择。通过将称为制冷剂的物质在其液体和气体形式之间转换，热泵代替加热空气，将热量从一个地方转移到另一个地方。您的冰箱是一台热泵。空调也是如此。这两个设备都反向泵送热量：制冷剂盘管吸收了热空气并将其抽出。冰箱和交流电源装置仅向一个方向移动热量。但是，热泵可以同时实现这两种功能，这意味着同一台设备可以在冬天（即使在非常寒冷的气候下）加热，而在夏天却可以凉爽。 （“热泵”是这些小工具的可怕名称，令人困惑。）
  热泵已经存在了几十年，但是它们过去却没有很高效，特别是在极端寒冷的天气下。情况正在改变。现在，一些冷气候热泵可以在低于零的温度下有效地传递热量。在最冷的日子里可能需要使用燃油或天然气为燃料的熔炉（或其他备用热源），但在其他所有日子中，您的热量都可以是电的。 
在缅因州，缺乏天然气基础设施使该州很容易鼓励家庭供暖电气化。在这种状态下，中央空气很少见，安装热泵可以免费增加冷气调节。缅因州的电网已经非常干净，这些新的热泵设备比窗户式交流电设备效率更高。
 该州的能源效率组织“效率缅因州信托基金会”的执行董事迈克尔·斯托达德（Michael Stoddard）告诉我，过去7年中，已有超过60,000台热泵出售给了Mainers。取暖油这种价格波动的商品价格高昂，烧毁了一些缅因州。国家资助的消费者回扣计划，其中包括一项提供高达1,500美元的热泵购买回扣的计划，也推动了设备的近期采用。斯托达德担心参加该州的奖励计划可能会枯竭，因为人们不想在大流行期间花钱。他说：“相反，参与人数增加了一倍。”人们被困在家里，其中一些人由于其刺激性福利和减少的支出而有多余的余钱。夏季，缅因州的部分地区仍可达到华氏90度。
  减碳本身是否会促使Mainers采用热泵？ “我相信答案是不断变化的，”斯托达德说。但是，即使居民没有考虑脱碳问题做出绿色能源的选择，诸如缅因州效率信托基金（Effective Maine Trust）之类的激励计划的成功也帮助该州提出了更积极的政策建议。斯托达德告诉我们，在我们谈话之前的几周内，缅因州刚刚完成了一项新的气候行动计划，而对脱碳供暖系统进行的减排是其三大建议之一。他说：“现在，每个人都在谈论这件事，好像这只是我们必须继续进行的事情。”
  E热泵的销售难度更大。天然气的污染少于馏出物，并且不受商品价格波动的影响，该价格波动使房主不再使用东北地区的取暖油。制造HVAC系统的Trane Technologies首席技术和战略官Paul Camuti表示，美国60％以上的住宅市场已经装有空调。这意味着从热泵切换中增加空调的好处并不适用于许多美国房主。
  即便如此，电力公司还是有强烈的动机促使房主转为电热：一方面，他们可以向他们出售更多的电。他们可以更快地实现自己的脱碳目标。出于上述两个原因，位于加利福尼亚州中部的萨克拉曼多市政公用事业区（SMUD）对热泵采取了美国最积极的回扣措施，其中热泵太空加热器最高可抵扣3,000美元，热泵热水器最高可抵扣2,500美元。 SMUD的电气化和能源效率战略业务规划师Scott Blunk认为，这些激励措施几乎可以立即收回投资。
 水加热计划一直是最受欢迎的，可能是因为该激励措施以最低的成本为房主和承包商提供了最大的利益。许多人仍然不熟悉使水和空间供热电气化的问题，Blunk推测给他们一个尝试该技术的理由可能会使他们像其他气电转化一样升温。在加利福尼亚州中部，热泵空间供暖是完全可行的，那里的温度不会经常降到零度以下。即使没有激励措施，从长远来看，用一个热泵代替空调装置和熔炉也可以节省很多钱，因为热泵可以同时起到两者的作用。
  与缅因州一样，萨克拉曼多正在利用其电气化计划的成功来改变公司战略和国家政策。目前，加利福尼亚州正在制定2023年能源法规，这使电子设备的推出变得更加容易。调整合理能源需求的基准线可以为更多家用设备电气化腾出空间。使建筑商更容易安装电器，鼓励他们推荐更清洁的电器。除了直接减少碳排放外，每台热泵装置都对政策变化的可行性产生增量影响。 
您也不必立即清除旧设备。 “要达到我们的脱碳目标，我们不需要拿出一个完美的热水器，”布隆克告诉我。 “当它熄灭时，我们只需要用一个电动的替代它即可。”对于房主而言，这是一个更容易吞下的药丸，他们可以将电力转换的增量成本视为比他们反正要花在更换设备上的钱少的溢价。回扣和激励措施使这笔交易变甜了。
  萨克拉曼多的电力不燃烧煤炭，并且无碳运行大约50％，这在很大程度上要归功于水力发电。 Blunk估算，在电网上新建的房屋每年可能会将其碳排放量从2.5吨减少到1.1吨，而1978年的房屋的碳排放量可能会从5.2吨减少到2.5吨。由于该地区80％的人口使用天然气进行空间和水加热，因此电气化可以大大减少那里的化石燃料使用量。
 不幸的是，许多美国人仍然不相信电力和可再生能源来加热。一些人将本月在一场严重风暴中德克萨斯州的普遍停电归咎于风力涡轮机的故障，但这是不对的：该州仍主要依靠天然气能源发电。甚至燃气炉也需要电才能工作，因此，如果长时间断电，这些设备的可靠性也不会降低，就像该州大部分地区的情况一样。在气候变化中，天然气不仅解决了另一个问题，还不能解决所有问题。能源与政策研究所执行董事戴维·波美兰兹（David Pomerantz）告诉我：“在使建筑物和车辆通电的同时，使用气候安全的清洁电力，同时使我们的基础设施适应不断变化的气候，将是极富挑战性的。” “但是在不断变化的气候中依靠天然气也将具有极大的挑战性。”电力是我们所做工作的基础，这使得对电网及其使用工具的广泛改革变得更加紧迫。
  我赞美电动汽车。在今年的超级碗比赛中，通用汽车公司花费数百万美元制作了星光熠熠的广告，以庆祝其雄心勃勃的电动汽车计划。令人惊讶，但并非不合时宜。毫不奇怪，在大型游戏中没有热泵广告播出。即使可以提高对电网的信任度，电热也面临一个大问题：用天然气进行转换并不像太阳能电池板或电动汽车那样性感。除非您是承包商或HVAC书呆子，否则您可能不会对您的供暖和制冷系统考虑太多。它们隐藏在阁楼，地下室和公用厕所中，藏在屋顶或院子里。除非它们崩溃了，否则这些机器几乎完全没有被考虑。当朋友来他们可能会在车库里炫耀特斯拉的方式时，没人会炫耀他们的新热水器。
 与太阳能电池板不同，家电的全新升级也不会产生社交信号带来的好处-邻居不能凝视您的绿色住宅，也不能为路人引以为傲。您如何使热泵性感？ “我不知道。”勃朗克承认。 “我认为我们最接近的是烹饪。”他指的是炉灶的蓝色火焰，这是住宅中居民在工作中唯一可以看到，听到和感觉到天然气的地方。炉灶烹饪对于证明家庭天然气服务的合理性至关重要，化石燃料行业已投入大量资源来保持家用电器的吸引力。
  甚至SMUD的高管都感到对厨房煤气的保护。 “您永远不会摆脱我的燃气灶，”勃朗克回忆说。因此，他购买了它们的便携式电磁炉（一种将热量直接传递到炊具的电炉），以证明现代的电烹饪热并不像他们可能会记得的1950年代的旧线圈。
 天然气烹饪仅占住宅天然气使用量的2％，远远少于空间和水的加热。但是，从燃气灶台转换为电磁炉仍会产生很大的影响（如果有所不同）。一方面，它对健康有好处：在家中点燃明火会产生污染，加剧哮喘。但是从可持续性的角度来看，厨房是人们与天然气建立情感联系的地方。坚固的铸铁炉ates上的蓝色火焰为烹饪赋予力量和控制感，就像肌肉车上的化油器的隆隆声对驾驶的影响一样。 
这使得电磁炉成为天然气脱碳运动的特斯拉。这是您可以吹嘘的设备，同时还可以向您的朋友和家人表明，用电就像做饭一样好，甚至更好。 “当我第一次得到归纳时，”勃朗克告诉我，“我参加了一个聚会，邀请了我所有的朋友过来。”
 Brian Keane围绕这个想法建立了一个整个组织。 SmartPower是一家非营利性可再生能源推广和营销公司，可帮助市政当局和公共事业提高其公民和客户对清洁能源的兴趣。 “美国人听听朋友和同事在做什么。那是压力点，”基恩告诉我。在能源方面，一旦采取了行动，无论是安装智能恒温器还是更换燃烧炉，最好的办法就是告诉朋友或同事您已经做到了。基恩说，这对于家庭中的可再生能源尤为重要，因为没有品牌名称与之相关。没有人知道他们有什么炉子或热水器。 “没有可口可乐，没有百事可乐。”
  SmartPower在全国范围内开展了一系列推广太阳能推广的营销活动，其中许多活动都是资金更充裕，形式更正式的Blunk感应炉家庭聚会。有时，当面板在附近的第一个屋顶上时，他们会举行集体聚会，或者举办带有公用事业账单的房客聚会（真的！）。人们过去曾经希望等待技术的普及，但是基恩认为生活方式技术的更新已经进入了家庭。他告诉房主：“苹果总是在推出新的iPhone。”热水器也一样。 “您可以继续等待，也可以直接购买。有效期为20年。”平均来看，通过SmartPower的太阳能计划，该市的太阳能采用率提高了近1000％。
  特灵（Trane）暖通空调设备主管Camuti认为，过去十年来人们对加热和冷却的看法发生了巨大变化。他告诉我：“我直接将其与在线信息的获取联系起来。”人们仍然问经销商，商人或承包商应该买什么，这使得从州和公用事业公司向那些代理商的上游激励变得极为重要。 “但是人们上网询问是否有更好的选择，”卡穆蒂说。 “我们开始与他们建立关系。”
  对于房主有一些教训。首先，倡导在您的街区，工作场所和家庭中进行家庭升级是有好处的。有些人出于自己的利益对清洁能源感兴趣，但更多的人是通过为自己的社区做些有益的事情而获得动力的。减排对于全球环境之前的本地环境产生了积极影响。其次，影响较小但更明显的升级，例如电磁炉或太阳能热水器，可能会成为放弃天然气的门户。毕竟，没有人认为他们想要或需要连接互联网的恒温器。但是它既时尚又实用，并且许多智能家居的推出都是从这个不太可能的开始开始的。
 如果您要盖新房，则完全跳过天然气会更容易。不运行天然气管线会节省数千美元，并且会阻止您安装天然气燃烧设备。但是对于现有房屋，您应该放弃太阳能屋顶或电动汽车是唯一的前进道路的想法。更换您的燃气灶具，并向朋友或Instagram展示。关于它的博客或播客，或在Facebook上发布，以便人们搜寻自己的产品时，搜索结果中会出现人类体验。如果您的煤气炉，空调或热水器的使用寿命即将结束，请用热泵更换它。如果没有，计划在它发出声音时这样做。如果您住在人口众多的中心，则应尽早采取这些措施，因为您的举动会向公用事业和政府发出更快的信号，表明趋势正在扭转。如果您想为减少碳排放做出自己的贡献，请尽力减少自己在家中使用的天然气的消耗，而不仅仅是您在汽车中使用的那种。
note_en:
I you know that getting rid of your car is one of the best things you can do for the climate, and also that you will never do it. This is a car-oriented country, and a  car-oriented time. But in 2019, the private cars and light trucks that ordinary people drive for work and shopping and leisure were responsible for  about 15 percent of U.S. fossil-fuel-energy use. Electric vehicles get a lot of press, but less than 1 percent of energy used for transportation came from electricity. Personal transportation is a large contributor to carbon emissions in America; it’s also the hardest to give up.
   But trading a gasoline automobile for an electric one (or for a bus or train) isn’t the only way ordinary citizens can contribute to fossil-fuel reduction. Decarbonization has two pillars: First, generate electricity from energy that does not emit carbon—renewable sources such as wind, solar, and geothermal instead of fossil fuels. That requires legislative and regulatory change. Second, use electricity to run as much of your personal life as possible.
 That’s where ordinary people like you and me can contribute. At least 7 percent of U.S. fossil-fuel energy is used for something fairly banal: residential space and water heating. Put differently, making relatively smaller, cheaper, and easy changes to home heating in America could reduce fossil-fuel use nearly as much as taking half of all private vehicles off the roads. If you want to do the most immediate good for the planet, replace your aging gas furnace with a new, electric appliance.
  Y  in many ways. A boiler heats water and cycles it through radiators that heat rooms. A furnace transfers heat to air, which it then pushes through vents into living spaces.
  Most American homes run these devices by burning fossil fuels. Depending on your geographic location and the age of your home and its systems, those fuels might include distillate fuel oil (mostly still used in the Northeast), propane (common in rural areas), or natural gas (common everywhere else). Every one of these releases carbon dioxide into the atmosphere when burned.
 But boilers and furnaces aren’t your only options. Instead of heating the air, heat pumps move heat from one place to another by converting a substance called refrigerant between its liquid and gas forms. Your refrigerator is a heat pump. So is an air conditioner. Both of those devices pump heat in reverse: Warm air is absorbed by the refrigerant coils and pumped out. Your fridge and AC unit move heat in only one direction. But a heat pump can do both, meaning that the same appliance can heat in the winter—even in very cold climates—and cool in the summer. (“Heat pump” is a terrible, confusing name for these gadgets.)
  Heat pumps have been around for decades, but they didn’t used to be very efficient, especially in extremely cold weather. That’s changing. Now some cold-climate heat pumps can transfer heat effectively in subzero temperatures. An oil- or gas-fueled furnace (or  other backup heat sources) might be required on the coldest days, but on all the others, your heat can be electric.
 In Maine, the lack of natural-gas infrastructure made it easy for the state to encourage electrification of home heating. Central air is uncommon in the state, and installing a heat pump adds cool air-conditioning for free. Maine’s electricity grid is already  very clean, and these new heat-pump devices are much more efficient than window AC units.
 Michael Stoddard, the executive director of the Efficiency Maine Trust, the state’s energy-efficiency organization, told me that more than 60,000 heat pumps have been sold to Mainers in the past seven years. Some Mainers have been burned by the high cost of heating oil, a commodity whose price fluctuates. State-sponsored consumer-rebate programs, including one that offers up to $1,500 back on purchases of heat pumps, has also driven recent adoption of the devices. Stoddard worried that participation in the state’s incentive programs might dry up because people wouldn’t want to spend the money during the pandemic. “Instead, participation has doubled,” he said. People were stuck at home, some with extra money to spare, given their stimulus benefits and reduced spending. And parts of Maine can still reach 90 degrees Fahrenheit in the summer.
  Does carbon reduction itself motivate Mainers to adopt heat pumps? “I’m confident the answer is that it is evolving,” Stoddard hedged. But even if residents aren’t making green-energy choices with decarbonization in mind, the success of incentive programs such as the Efficiency Maine Trust’s have helped the state advance more aggressive policy proposals. In the weeks prior to our conversation, Stoddard told me, Maine had just completed a new climate action plan, and decarbonizing heating systems was among its top three mitigation recommendations. “Now you have everybody talking about this as if it’s just a thing we have to get going on,” he said.
  E  a heat pump is a tougher sell. Natural gas pollutes less than distillate, and it doesn’t suffer the commodity-price fluctuations that have helped shift homeowners off heating oil in the Northeast. And more than 60 percent of the U.S. residential market already has air-conditioning, according to Paul Camuti, the chief technology and strategy officer at Trane Technologies, which manufactures HVAC systems. That means the benefit of added air-conditioning from a heat-pump switchover doesn’t apply to many American homeowners.
  Even so, electric utilities have strong incentives to move homeowners to electric heat: They can sell them more electricity, for one thing. And they can realize their own decarbonization goals more rapidly. For both of these reasons, the Sacramento Municipal Utility District (SMUD) in central California has adopted some of the country’s most aggressive rebate incentives for heat pumps, as much as $3,000 on heat-pump space heaters and $2,500 on heat-pump water heaters. According to Scott Blunk, SMUD’s strategic business planner of electrification and energy efficiency, the incentives can make the payback almost immediate.
 The water-heating program has been the most popular, probably because the incentive provides the most benefit at the lowest cost to homeowners and contractors. Electrifying water and space heating is still unfamiliar to many people, and Blunk speculated that giving them a reason to try the technology might warm them up, as it were, to other gas-to-electric conversions. Heat-pump space heating is entirely viable in central California, where the temperature doesn’t drop below freezing that often. Even without the incentives, replacing an air-conditioning unit and a furnace with one heat pump can save a lot of money in the long run, since the heat pump can do the job of both.
  As was the case in Maine, Sacramento is leveraging the success of its electrification programs to shift corporate strategy and state policy. The energy code for 2023 is currently being developed in California, and it is making the rollout of electric devices easier. Adjusting the  baselines of reasonable energy need can make room for electrifying more home devices. And making electric-appliance installations easier for builders encourages them to recommend cleaner appliances. In addition to reducing carbon emissions directly, every heat-pump installation has an incremental effect on the viability of policy changes.
 You don’t have to get rid of your old appliances right away, either. “To hit our decarbonization goals, we don’t need to take out someone’s perfectly good water heater,” Blunk told me. “We just need to replace it with an electric one when it goes out.” That’s an easier pill to swallow for homeowners, who can think of the incremental cost of electric conversions as a small premium over the money they were going to spend on a replacement device anyway. Rebates and incentives sweeten the deal.
  Sacramento power burns no coal, and runs roughly 50 percent carbon-free, thanks in large part to hydroelectric power. Blunk calculates that a new-construction home on the grid might reduce its carbon output from 2.5 to 1.1 tons of carbon a year, and a 1978 home’s could drop from 5.2 to 2.5 tons. Because 80 percent of the region uses natural gas for space and water heating, electrification could substantially reduce fossil-fuel use there.
 Unfortunately, many Americans still don’t trust electric and renewable sources for heating. Some blamed Texas’s widespread outages during a severe storm this month on the failure of wind turbines, but  that’s not right: The state  still relies largely on natural-gas energy for electricity. And even gas-burning furnaces require electricity to work, making those appliances no less unreliable if the power goes out for an extended time, as it did across much of the state. Amid climate change, gas isn’t an answer so much as just another problem. “Electrifying buildings and vehicles  while switching to climate-safe clean electricity  while adapting our infrastructure to a changing climate will be deeply challenging,” David Pomerantz, the executive director of the Energy and Policy Institute, told me. “But relying on gas in a changing climate would  also be deeply challenging.” Electric power is fundamental to everything we do, making widespread reform of the grid and the tools that use it even more urgent.
  I  glorifying electric cars. For this year’s Super Bowl, General Motors spent millions on a star-studded ad  celebrating its ambitious electric-vehicle plans. It was surprising, but not out of place. Less surprisingly, no heat-pump ads aired during the big game. Even if trust in the grid can be improved, electric heat faces one big problem: Transitioning off natural gas just isn’t as sexy as solar panels or electric cars. Unless you’re a contractor or an HVAC nerd, you probably don’t think much about your heating and cooling systems. They are hidden in attics and basements and utility closets, tucked away on roofs or in side yards. These machines go almost entirely unconsidered unless they break down. Nobody shows off their new water heater when friends come over the way they might show off a Tesla in the garage.
 Unlike solar panels, clean upgrades to home appliances also don’t produce social-signaling benefits—the neighbors can’t gawk at your greener home, and you can’t take pride in passersby noticing it. How do you make a heat pump sexy? “I don’t know,” Blunk admitted. “I think the closest we have is cooking.” He means the blue flame of a stove, the only place in the home where a resident can see and hear and feel natural gas at work. Stove-top cooking is so essential to justifying home gas service, the fossil-fuel industry has  poured resources into preserving the appliances’ appeal.
  Even SMUD’s executives felt protective of kitchen gas. “You’re never going to get rid of my gas stove,” Blunk recalled them saying. So he bought them portable induction-cooking units (a kind of electric stove that transfers heat directly to cookware) to demonstrate that modern electric cooking heat wasn’t like the old wire coils they might remember from the 1950s.
 Natural-gas cooking is responsible for only 2 percent of residential natural-gas use—far less than space and water heating. Still, converting from a gas cooktop to an electric-induction one can have a substantial, if different, impact. For one part, there are  health benefits: Igniting open-gas fires in your home produces pollution that can exacerbate asthma. But from a sustainability perspective, the kitchen is the place where people develop an emotional relationship to natural gas. The blue flame lapping over the sturdy cast-iron grates imparts a sense of power and control to cooking, just as the rumble of a carburetor on a muscle car does to driving.
  That makes the induction cooktop the Tesla of the natural-gas-decarbonization movement. It’s the device you can brag about while also showing your friends and family that electricity is just as good as gas for cooking, if not better. “When I first got my induction,” Blunk told me, “I had a party and invited all my friends over.”
 Brian Keane has built a whole organization around that idea. SmartPower, a nonprofit renewable-energy outreach and marketing company, helps municipalities and utilities get their citizens and customers interested in clean energy. “Americans listen to what friends and co-workers are doing. That’s the pressure point,” Keane told me. And when it comes to energy, once you’ve  taken action, whether that be installing a smart thermostat or replacing a combustion furnace, the best thing to do is tell a friend or colleague that you’ve done it. This is particularly important for renewable energy in the home, Keane said, because no brand name is associated with it. Nobody knows what furnace or water heater they have. “There’s no Coca Cola, no Pepsi.”
  SmartPower has run a series of marketing campaigns for solar adoption around the country, many of which amount to better-funded and more-formal versions of Blunk’s induction-stove house party. Sometimes they hold block parties when the panels go up on the first roof in a neighborhood, or host house parties with a utility-bill reveal (really!). People used to want to wait for a technology to be widespread, but Keane thinks that lifestyle-technology turnover has entered the home. “Apple is always coming out with a new iPhone,” he tells homeowners; a water heater is no different. “You could keep waiting, or just buy this one. It’ll work for 20 years.” Averaging across campaigns, SmartPower’s solar programs were found to increase the rate of solar adoptions in a municipality by nearly 1,000 percent.
  Camuti, the Trane HVAC-equipment executive, agrees that the change in perception about heating and cooling in the past decade has been dramatic. “I relate this directly to the availability of online information,” he told me. People still ask dealers, tradespeople, or contractors what they should buy, which makes upstream incentives from states and utilities to those agents extremely important. “But people go online and ask if there is a better choice,” Camuti said. “We are starting to have a relationship with them.”
  There are some lessons for homeowners. First, there is benefit in advocating for home upgrades on your block, at your workplace, and in your family. Some people are interested in clean energy for its own sake, but many more are motivated by doing something beneficial for their communities. And emissions reductions positively impact a local environment before the global one. Second, lower-impact but more-visible upgrades, such as induction cooktops or solar water heaters, might become the gateway to abandoning natural gas. Nobody thought they wanted or needed an internet-connected thermostat before Nest came on the scene, after all. But it was stylish and functional, and many smart-home rollouts began from that unlikely start.
 If you’re building a new home, it’s easier to skip natural gas entirely. Not running a gas line will save thousands of dollars, and it will prevent you from ever installing gas-burning appliances. But for existing homes, you should abandon the idea that a solar roof or an electric car is the only path forward. Replace your gas cooktop and show it off to friends, or on Instagram. Blog or podcast or post on Facebook about it, so that human experiences will come up in search results when people go hunting for their own products. If your gas furnace or air conditioner or water heater is near the end of its lifespan, replace it with a heat pump. And if not, plan to do so when it conks out. If you live in a large population center, do these things sooner, because your action will send faster signals to utilities and governments that the tide is turning. If you want to do your part to reduce carbon emissions, do what you can to wean yourself off the gas you use in your home, not just the kind you put in your car.
title: 热泵是房主应对气候变化的最简单方法之一
title_en: Heat pumps are one of the easiest ways for homeowners to fight climate change

由于担心遭到报复，应大屠杀的所有证人的要求，都给他们取了化名。
      这些尸体，有些穿着白色教堂长袍，鲜血淋漓，散落在干旱的田野，矮小的农田和干燥的河床中。其他人的双手被绑在皮带上，被枪杀在他们的家门口。目击者，父母及其老师说，死者中有牧师，老人，妇女，整个家庭以及一群20多名主日学童，其中一些年仅14岁。
 亚伯拉罕立即认出了其中一些孩子。他们来自埃塞俄比亚北部提格雷地区埃达加·哈穆斯（Edaga Hamus）的小镇，两周前也逃到那里。冲突激化后，亚伯拉罕和他的家人以及其他数百名流离失所者逃到了登格莱特（Dengelat），该村庄位于崎steep不平的山谷中，周围是陡峭，锈色的悬崖环绕。他们在Maryam Dengelat寻求庇护，Maryam Dengelat是一座历史悠久的修道院建筑群，以一座拥有百年历史的凿岩教堂而闻名。
     11月30日，数十名宗教朝圣者参加了Tsion Maryam东正教节日，这是一年一度的盛宴，以纪念埃塞俄比亚人相信约柜是从耶路撒冷被带到该国的日子。在数周的暴力冲突中，圣洁的日子是令人欣慰的，但它不会持续。
  目击者说，一群厄立特里亚士兵向Maryam Dengelat教堂开火，而数百名全民则在庆祝弥撒。人们试图步行逃离，爬上通往邻近村庄的悬崖小路。部队跟在后面，向山腰喷上子弹。
 CNN的调查取材于12位目击者，20多名幸存者的亲属的采访以及摄影证据，为接下来发生的事情提供了启示。
 士兵们挨家挨户，将人们拖离家园。母亲被迫捆绑儿子。一名孕妇被枪杀，丈夫被杀。一些幸存者躲在死者的尸体下。 
这场混乱持续了三天，士兵们屠杀了当地居民，流离失所者和朝圣者。最终，在12月2日，士兵们允许进行非正式的葬礼，但威胁要杀害所有他们哀悼的人。亚伯拉罕自愿参加。
 美国有线电视新闻网（CNN）获得的录像显示了在登格拉特（Dengelat）遇难者的鞋子。信用：由CNN获得
  在他们观察的目光下，他整理着儿童和青少年的尸体时，泪流满面，从口袋里收集身份证，并对他们的衣服或发型做细致的记录。亚伯拉罕说，有些是完全被认出的，被枪杀了。
 然后，他用泥土和棘手的树枝遮住了他们的尸体，祈求它们不会被冲走，也不会被鬣狗和秃鹰盘旋而被带走。最终，他把他们的鞋子放在了土墩上，这样他就可以和他们的父母一起回去认出他们。
  ＆＃34;他们的手被绑住了……年幼的孩子们……我们到处都看到了他们。路上有一个老人被杀，一个八十多岁的男人。还有他们在露天杀害的年幼孩子。我从未见过这样的大屠杀，我也不想[再次]亚伯拉罕说。
  亚伯拉罕说，他当天埋葬了50多人，但估计有100多人在袭击中丧生。
   自11月以来，数千名平民被杀害。自11月埃塞俄比亚总理阿比·艾哈迈德（Abiy Ahmed）因解决与邻国厄立特里亚的长期冲突而获得2019年诺贝尔和平奖以来，他们发起了针对提格雷地区的政党。他指责提格雷人民解放阵线（TPLF）攻击政府军事基地并试图窃取武器，该阵线在埃比埃于2018年上任前统治埃塞俄比亚近三十年。 TPLF拒绝了该索赔。 
冲突是双方之间紧张局势升级的最终结果，也是非洲第二人口大国最近发生的几次民族民族主义冲突中最可怕的一次。
 在11月下旬夺取了提格雷的主要城市的控制权后，阿比宣告获胜，并坚持认为没有任何平民在这次进攻中受到伤害。阿比还否认厄立特里亚士兵越过提格里来支持埃塞俄比亚部队。
   但是战斗在农村和山区展开，据报道，特遣部队及其武装支持者躲藏在那里，抵制阿比（Abiy）巩固权力的努力。暴力事件已蔓延到当地社区，在交火中俘虏平民，并触发了联合国难民署所谓的二十年来难民从该地区逃离的最惨重的旅程。
 联合国预防种族灭绝问题特别顾问在2月初表示，该组织已收到多起关于“法外处决，性暴力，抢劫，大规模处决和人道主义准入受到阻碍的报道”。
 这些虐待中有许多被归咎于厄立特里亚士兵，他们在实地的存在表明阿比与厄立特里亚总统以赛亚·阿弗韦基的备受赞誉的和平协议为双方发动了对TPLF的战争-他们的相互敌人。
 美国国务院在给美国有线电视新闻网（CNN）的声明中，呼吁厄立特里亚部队立即从提格雷（Tigray）撤出，引用了有关他们参与“令人不安的行为”的可信报告。发言人在回应CNN的调查结果时说，玛丽安·登格拉特（Maryam Dengelat）发生的大屠杀报告令人严重关切，需要进行独立调查。
 埃塞俄比亚对CNN的置评请求作出回应，该声明未直接解决在登格拉特（Dengelat）的袭击事件。政府表示，将在对该地区涉嫌犯罪进行彻底调查后，将继续将所有肇事者绳之以法。但没有提供有关这些调查的详细信息。 
美国有线电视新闻网（CNN）已向厄立特里亚（Eritrea）征求评论，但尚未作出回应。国际特赦组织报道，星期五，政府坚决否认其士兵在提格雷发生另一场屠杀期间犯下了暴行。
 TPLF在给CNN的声明中说，大屠杀发生时，它的部队不在Dengelat附近。它拒绝认为受害者可能被误认为是TPLF，并呼吁联合国进行调查，以使各方对冲突期间犯下的暴行负责。
 尽管如此，该国内部的局势仍然不透明。埃塞俄比亚政府严格限制了与记者的接触，并阻止大多数援助到达政府控制范围之外的地区，这使得核实幸存者的账目变得颇具挑战性。战斗期间间歇性的通信中断有效地阻止了这场战争。
 现在，随着逃离提格里部分地区的目击者可以访问互联网并恢复了电话线，窗帘被拉回了。他们详细介绍了一场灾难性的冲突，该冲突引起了种族暴力，包括对教堂和清真寺的袭击。
 数月以来，谣言四起，对登格拉特的东正教教堂进行了严厉的袭击。死者名单于12月初开始在社交媒体上流传，在Tigrayan散居海外的人中分享。然后，死者的照片，包括年幼的孩子，开始在网上出现。
 通过活动家和亲戚的网络，CNN追踪了目击者的目击。在无数的电话中-许多人断线和掉线-亚伯拉罕和其他人提供了迄今为止致命屠杀的最详尽的描述。
   目击者说，这个节日与其他年份一样开始了很多。 2019年庆祝活动的录像显示，祭司身着白色礼袍和王冠，高举十字架，带领数百人在Maryam Dengelat教堂祈祷。忠实的歌声一致地唱歌，跳舞和跳舞。 
当祈祷在11月30日凌晨结束时，亚伯拉罕从教堂所在的山顶上望出去，看到部队步行到达，随后有更多的士兵乘坐卡车。他说，起初，他们是和平的。邀请他们吃饭，并在树荫下休息。
 但是，正当整群人庆祝中午弥撒之际，炮击和枪声突然爆发，使人们逃离山路进入附近的房屋。
 德斯塔为节日的筹备工作提供了帮助，他说，当部队到达村庄入口时，他正在教堂，封锁道路并开枪。他听到人们尖叫并逃跑，在Ziqallay山腰上奔跑。从多岩石的高原上，他观察了下面发生的混乱情况。
 我们可以看到有人在这里和那里跑来跑去……[士兵]杀死了所有从教堂来的人，＆＃34;德斯塔说。
 八名目击者说，根据他们的制服和方言，他们可以说这支部队是厄立特里亚人。一些人推测，士兵们是针对青年男子的报复，假设他们是TPLF部队或当地同盟民兵的成员。但是亚伯拉罕和其他人坚持认为，登格拉特（Dengelat）或教堂里没有民兵。
 玛塔（Marta）假期前往登格拉特（Dengelat）度假，她说她在早上祈祷后与丈夫比尼亚姆（Biniam）离开教堂。当新婚夫妇回到他们的亲戚家时，一群人开始冲刺上山，大喊着士兵正在村里围捕人们。
   她回忆起士兵们到达他们的房子，向大院开枪并大喊一声的恐怖时刻：＆＃34;出来，出来给你打招呼。＆＃34;玛尔塔（Marta）说，他们高举身份证出门，说我们是平民。但是无论如何，部队开了火，击中了Biniam，他的妹妹和其他几个人。 
我抱着比尼，他没死……我以为他会活下来，但他死在了我的怀里。
 这对夫妇刚刚在十月份结婚。玛塔在大屠杀后发现她怀孕了。
 士兵们离开后，称自己被枪杀的玛尔塔（Marta）帮助将七个尸体拖入其中，以免鬣狗吃掉它们。我们睡在尸体附近，因为他们[士兵]还在那里，所以我们无法掩埋他们，＆＃34;她说。
   玛尔塔（Marta）和其他目击者描述了士兵们通过登格拉特（Dengelat）挨家挨户，将人们拖到外面，束缚他们的手或要求他人这样做，然后开枪射击他们。
 拉瓦（Rahwa）是埃达加·哈穆斯（Edaga Hamus）的星期日学校小组的成员，比其他人更早离开登格拉特（Dengelat），设法逃脱了被杀的命运。他说，母亲被迫绑上自己的儿子。
 他们正在命令母亲绑儿子。手。他们赤脚地把他们带走，并在母亲面前杀死他们，＆＃34;拉瓦说目击者告诉她。
 另一位目击者塞缪尔（Samuel）说，他在士兵们来到教堂后面的他的房子之前，已经与他们一起吃饭和喝酒，并杀死了他的亲戚。他说，他通过躲藏在他们的尸体下面几个小时而得以幸存。 
＆＃34;他们开始将人们赶出家门，并且正在杀死所有儿童，妇女和老人。他们在房屋外杀死他们后，正在抢劫并抢走所有财产，＆＃34;塞缪尔说。
 暴力肆虐之时，数百人仍留在教堂大厅里。牧师在枪声中平静下来，劝告那些可以回家的人，将他们带到外面。亚伯拉罕说，有几位神父在离开教堂时被杀。
 由于无处可逃，亚伯拉罕躲在Maryam Dengelat内，躺在炮弹撞击铁皮屋顶的地板上。 ＆＃34;我们失去了希望，我们决定留在教堂里死去。我们没有尝试运行，他说。
 两天后，部队从教堂召集教区居民来对付死者。亚伯拉罕说，他和其他五名男子整日埋葬尸体，包括玛塔家和周日学童的尸体。但是，部队禁止他们按照东正教的传统将尸体埋在教堂里，并强迫他们改做万人冢-这种做法在提格雷的其他地方都有描述。
   亚伯拉罕分享了这些墓地的照片和视频，在几位专家的卫星图像分析的帮助下，CNN将这些遗址和照片地理定位在登格拉特。该分析无法最终确定单个坟墓，目击者称这些坟墓很浅，但一位专家表示，有迹象表明部分景观已发生变化。
 亚伯拉罕说，最初的流血事件发生后是两个紧张星期。在最终继续前进之前，士兵们在该地区数个营地中停留，偷车，焚烧农作物并杀死牲畜。
 特德罗斯（Tedros）出生在登格拉特（Dengelat），在士兵们离开后曾去过那里。他说，这个村庄闻到了死亡的气息，秃鹰在山上盘旋，这表明那里可能还有更多的尸体被遗忘了。 
＆＃34;其中一些人在试图逃跑的同时也被杀害。他们中的大多数人在被埋葬之前被秃v吞噬，这太可怕了。 [士兵们]绑住他们，并杀死他们在门前，然后将他们开枪射中头部以保存子弹，＆＃34;他说。
 特德罗斯（Tedros）参观了目击者描述的墓地，并说他在教堂墙壁上发现了大炮击中的裂缝。在与村民和家庭成员的访谈中，他汇总了70多人的死亡人数。
 他们的家人希望，特德罗斯，亚伯拉罕和其他人冒着生命危险记录下来的亲人的名字最终将在玛丽安姆·登格拉特教堂的传统葬礼上宣读，这在正在进行的冲突中很少发生。
 屠杀发生三个月后，登格拉特（Dengelat）的坟墓每天提醒着留在村里的幸存者的流血事件。但是，它还不够安全，无法掩埋死者的尸体，而现实正在使他们沉重。
     更正：该故事中视频的早期版本中的屏幕标题误以为从Tigray获得CNN素材的日期错了。它是在2021年拍摄的。
 伊丽莎·麦金托什（Eliza Mackintosh）撰写并报道。据报道，Barbara Arvanitidis，Nima Elbagir，伯利恒Feleke，Gianluca Mezzofiore和Katie Polglase。尼克·汤普森（Nick Thompson）编辑。视频和社论由丹·赖特（Dan Wright）监督。 Peter Robertson，Henrik Pettersson，Brett Roegiers，Sarah Tilotta，Temujin Doran和Lauren Cook的设计和视觉编辑。詹妮弗·汉斯勒（Jennifer Hansler）为华盛顿特区的这份报告做出了贡献。
note_en:
All of the witnesses to this massacre have been given pseudonyms at their request due to fears of retribution.
      The corpses, some dressed in white church robes drenched in blood, were scattered in arid fields, scrubby farmlands and a dry riverbed. Others had been shot on their doorsteps with their hands bound with belts. Among the dead were priests, old men, women, entire families and a group of more than 20 Sunday school children, some as young as 14, according to eyewitnesses, parents and their teacher.
 Abraham recognized some of the children immediately. They were from his town in Ethiopia's northern Tigray region, Edaga Hamus, and had also fled fighting there two weeks earlier. As clashes raged, Abraham and his family, along with hundreds of other displaced people, escaped to Dengelat, a nearby village in a craggy valley ringed by steep, rust-colored cliffs. They sought shelter at Maryam Dengelat, a historic monastery complex famed for a centuries-old, rock-hewn church.
     On November 30, they were joined by scores of religious pilgrims for the Orthodox festival of Tsion Maryam, an annual feast to mark the day Ethiopians believe the Ark of the Covenant was brought to the country from Jerusalem. The holy day was a welcome respite from weeks of violence, but it would not last.
  A group of Eritrean soldiers opened fire on Maryam Dengelat church while hundreds of congregants were celebrating mass, eyewitnesses say. People tried to flee on foot, scrambling up cliff paths to neighboring villages. The troops followed, spraying the mountainside with bullets.
 A CNN investigation drawing on interviews with 12 eyewitnesses, more than 20 relatives of the survivors and photographic evidence sheds light on what happened next.
 The soldiers went door to door, dragging people from their homes. Mothers were forced to tie up their sons. A pregnant woman was shot, her husband killed. Some of the survivors hid under the bodies of the dead.
 The mayhem continued for three days, with soldiers slaughtering local residents, displaced people and pilgrims. Finally, on December 2, the soldiers allowed informal burials to take place, but threatened to kill anyone they saw mourning. Abraham volunteered.
 Footage obtained by CNN shows the shoes of some of those killed in Dengelat.  Credit: Obtained by CNN
  Under their watchful eyes, he held back tears as he sorted through the bodies of children and teenagers, collecting identity cards from pockets and making meticulous notes about their clothing or hairstyle. Some were completely unrecognizable, having been shot in the face, Abraham said.
 Then he covered their bodies with earth and thorny tree branches, praying that they wouldn't be washed away, or carried off by prowling hyenas and circling vultures. Finally he placed their shoes on top of the burial mounds, so he could return with their parents to identify them.
  "Their hands were tied ... young children ... we saw them everywhere. There was an elderly man who had been killed on the road, an 80-something-year-old man. And the young kids they killed on the street in the open. I've never seen a massacre like this and I don't want to [again]," Abraham said.
  Abraham said he buried more than 50 people that day, but estimates more than 100 died in the assault.
   They're among thousands of civilians believed to have been killed since November, when Ethiopian Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed, who was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2019 for resolving a long-running conflict with neighboring Eritrea,  launched a major military operation against the political party that governs the Tigray region. He accused the Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF), which ruled Ethiopia for nearly three decades before Abiy took office in 2018, of attacking a government military base and trying to steal weapons. The TPLF denies the claim.
 The conflict is the culmination of escalating tensions between the two sides, and the most dire of several recent ethno-nationalist clashes in Africa's second-most populous country.
 After seizing control of Tigray's main cities in late November, Abiy declared victory and maintained that  no civilians were harmed in the offensive. Abiy has also denied that soldiers from Eritrea crossed into Tigray to support Ethiopian forces.
   But the fighting has raged on in rural and mountainous areas where the TPLF and its armed supporters are reportedly hiding out, resisting Abiy's drive to consolidate power. The violence has spilled over into local communities, catching civilians in the crossfire and triggering what the  United Nations refugee agency has called the worst flight of refugees from the region in two decades.
 The  UN special adviser on genocide prevention said in early February that the organization had received multiple reports of "extrajudicial killings, sexual violence, looting, mass executions and impeded humanitarian access."
 Many of those abuses have been blamed on Eritrean soldiers, whose presence on the ground suggests that Abiy's much-lauded peace deal with Eritrean President Isaias Afwerki set the stage for the two sides to wage war against the TPLF -- their mutual enemy.
 The US State Department, in a statement to CNN, called for Eritrean forces to be "withdrawn from Tigray immediately," citing credible reports of their involvement in "deeply troubling conduct." In response to CNN's findings, the spokesperson said "reports of a massacre at Maryam Dengelat are gravely concerning and demand an independent investigation."
 Ethiopia responded to CNN's request for comment with a statement that did not directly address the attack in Dengelat. The government said it would "continue bringing all perpetrators to justice following thorough investigations into alleged crimes in the region," but gave no details about those investigations.
   CNN has reached out for comment to Eritrea, which has yet to respond. On Friday, the  government vehemently denied its soldiers had committed atrocities during another massacre in Tigray reported by Amnesty International.
 The TPLF said in a statement to CNN that its forces were nowhere near Dengelat at the time of the massacre. It rejected that the victims could have been mistaken for being TPLF and called for a UN investigation to hold all sides accountable for atrocities committed during the conflict.
 Still, the situation inside the country remains opaque. Ethiopia's government has severely restricted access to journalists and  prevented most aid from reaching areas beyond the government's control, making it challenging to verify accounts from survivors. And an intermittent communications blackout during the fighting has effectively blocked the war from the world's eyes.
 Now that curtain is being pulled back, as witnesses fleeing parts of Tigray reach internet access and phone lines are restored. They detail a disastrous conflict that has given rise to ethnic violence, including attacks on churches and mosques.
 For months, rumors spread of a grisly assault on an Orthodox church in Dengelat. A list of the dead began circulating on social media in early December, shared among the Tigrayan diaspora. Then photos of the deceased, including young children, started cropping up online.
 Through a network of activists and relatives, CNN tracked down eyewitnesses to the attack. In countless phone calls -- many disconnected and dropped -- Abraham and others provided the most detailed account of the deadly massacre to date.
   Eyewitnesses said that the festival started much as it had any other year. Footage of the celebrations from 2019 shows priests dressed in white ceremonial robes and crowns, carrying crosses aloft, leading hundreds of people in prayer at Maryam Dengelat church. The faithful sang, danced and ululated in unison.
 As prayers concluded in the early hours of November 30, Abraham looked out from the hilltop where the church is perched to see troops arriving by foot, followed by more soldiers in trucks. At first, they were peaceful, he said. They were invited to eat, and rested under the shade of a tree grove.
 But, as congregants were celebrating mass around midday, shelling and gunfire erupted, sending people fleeing up mountain paths and into nearby homes.
 Desta, who helped with preparations for the festival, said he was at the church when troops arrived at the village entrance, blocking off the road and firing shots. He heard people screaming and fled, running up Ziqallay mountainside. From the rocky plateau he surveyed the chaos playing out below.
 We could see people running here and there ... [the soldiers] were killing everyone who was coming from the church," Desta said.
 Eight eyewitnesses said they could tell the troops were Eritrean, based on their uniforms and dialect. Some speculated that soldiers were meting out revenge by targeting young men, assuming they were members of the TPLF forces or allied local militias. But Abraham and others maintained there were no militia in Dengelat or the church.
 Marta, who was visiting Dengelat for the holiday, says she left the church with her husband Biniam after morning prayers. As the newlyweds walked back to their relative's home, a stream of people began sprinting up the hill, shouting that soldiers were rounding people up in the village.
   She recalled the horrifying moment soldiers arrived at their house, shooting into the compound and calling out: "Come out, come out you b*tches." Marta said they went outside holding their identity cards aloft, saying "we're civilians." But the troops opened fire anyway, hitting Biniam, his sister and several others.
 "I was holding Bini, he wasn't dead ... I thought he was going to survive, but he died [in my arms].
 The couple had just been married in October. Marta found out after the massacre that she was pregnant.
 After the soldiers left, Marta, who said she was shot in the hand, helped drag the seven bodies inside, so that the hyenas wouldn't eat them. "We slept near the bodies ... and we couldn't bury them because they [the soldiers] were still there," she said.
   Marta and other eyewitnesses described soldiers going house to house through Dengelat, dragging people outside, binding their hands or asking others to do so, and then shooting them.
 Rahwa, who was part of the Sunday school group from Edaga Hamus and left Dengelat earlier than others, managing to escape being killed, said mothers were forced to tie up their sons.
 "They were ordering their mothers to tie their sons' hands. They were taking them barefoot and killing them in front of their mothers," Rahwa said eyewitnesses told her.
 Samuel, another eyewitness, said that he had eaten and drank with the soldiers before they came to his house, which is just behind the church, and killed his relatives. He said he survived by hiding underneath one of their bodies for hours.
 "They started pushing the people out of their houses and they were killing all children, women and old men. After they killed them outside their houses, they were looting and taking all the property," Samuel said.
 As the violence raged, hundreds of people remained in the church hall. In a lull in the gunfire, priests advised those who could to go home, ushering them outside. Several of the priests were killed as they left the church, Abraham said.
 With nowhere to run to, Abraham sheltered inside Maryam Dengelat, lying on the floor as artillery pounded the tin roof. "We lost hope and we decided to stay and die at the church. We didn't try to run," he said.
 Two days later, the troops called parishioners down from the church to deal with the dead. Abraham said he and five other men spent the day burying bodies, including those from Marta's household and the Sunday school children. But the troops forbid them from burying bodies at the church, in line with Orthodox tradition, and forced them to make mass graves instead -- a practice that has been described elsewhere in Tigray.
   Abraham shared photos and videos of the grave sites, which CNN geolocated to Dengelat with the help of satellite image analysis from several experts. The analysis was unable to conclusively identify individual graves, which witnesses said were shallow, but one expert said there were signs that parts of the landscape had changed.
 The initial bloodshed was followed by a period of two tense weeks, Abraham said. Soldiers stayed in the area in several encampments, stealing cars, burning crops and killing livestock before eventually moving on.
 Tedros, who was born in Dengelat and traveled there after the soldiers had left, said that the village smelled of death and that vultures were circling over the mountains, a sign that there may be more bodies left uncounted there.
 "Some of them were also killed in the far fields while they were trying to escape and most of them were eaten by vultures before they got buried, it was horrible. [The soldiers] tied them and killed them in front of their doors, and they shot them in the head just to save bullets," he said.
 Tedros visited the burial grounds described by eyewitnesses and said he saw cracks in the church walls where artillery hit. In interviews with villagers and family members, he compiled a death toll of more than 70 people.
 The families hope that the names of their loved ones, which Tedros, Abraham and others risked their lives to record, will eventually be read out at a traditional funeral ceremony at the Maryam Dengelat church -- rare closure in an ongoing conflict.
 Three months after the massacre, the graves in Dengelat are a daily reminder of the bloodshed for the survivors who remain in the village. But it has not yet been safe enough to rebury the bodies of those who died, and that reality is weighing on them.
     Correction: An on-screen title in an earlier version of the video in this story misstated the date of CNN-obtained footage from Tigray. It was filmed in 2021.
 Eliza Mackintosh wrote and reported. Barbara Arvanitidis, Nima Elbagir, Bethlehem Feleke, Gianluca Mezzofiore and Katie Polglase reported.   Edited by Nick Thompson. Video and editorial supervised by Dan Wright. Design and visual editing by Peter Robertson, Henrik Pettersson, Brett Roegiers, Sarah Tilotta, Temujin Doran and Lauren Cook.  Jennifer Hansler contributed to this report from Washington, DC.

有抱负的电动卡车制造商尼古拉（Nikola）已向证券交易委员会（Securities and Exchange Commission）承认，创始人特雷弗·米尔顿（Trevor Milton）的九项陈述不正确。谣言首次曝光后不久，弥尔顿于9月被迫从尼古拉辞职。
 在2016年至2020年之间，弥尔顿（Milton）向一系列购物者介绍了他刚起步的卡车制造商。在2016年的新闻发布会上，米尔顿登上舞台，揭开了该公司首款卡车的原型，该卡车被称为“尼古拉一号”。在活动期间，米尔顿声称这辆卡车完全运转了。实际上，尼古拉从来没有让卡车在自己的力量下移动。
  Nikola最臭名昭著的flimflam出现在2018年，当时该公司发布了Nikola One运动中的视频。实际上，尼古拉已经将无法操作的卡车拖到了一条长长的浅坡的顶部，然后将其向下滚动，使相机倾斜以使其看起来像是在水平地面上行驶。
 在一家名为Hindenburg Research的卖空公司发表了一份报告，宣布尼古拉为“大规模欺诈”之后，这些错误陈述和其他虚假陈述于9月份暴露出来。作为回应，尼古拉委托柯克兰和埃利斯律师事务所审查兴登堡的指控。尽管最终的报告尚未公开，但尼古拉现在已经对米尔顿（Milton）2020年9月离任前的九项不正确陈述做出了回应。他们包括：
 ＆＃34;在2016年7月，公司表示拥有天然气井的所有权，并在2016年8月，将这些井用作太阳能氢气生产的备用设备。
 ＆＃34;在2019年4月，米尔顿表示，公司总部屋顶上的太阳能电池板每天产生约18兆瓦的能源。
 ＆＃34;在2019年12月和2020年7月，米尔顿表示公司可以生产＆＃39;公司演示站的氢气超过1,000公斤，并且公司低于＆＃39;当时$ 3 /公斤＆＃34; 
Nikola现在承认，这些陈述在制作时全部或部分不准确。
 尼古拉说，柯克兰和埃利斯尚未做出虚假陈述，决定该公司是否违反任何法律。该公司的调查正在进行中。
 尼古拉说，由于这些错误陈述，它仍可能面临严重的法律后果。联邦检察官和证券交易委员会都对去年9月的情况产生了兴趣。尼古拉说，它无法预测这些调查将如何进行。根据与特雷弗·弥尔顿（Trevor Milton）达成的赔偿协议，尼古拉已产生800万美元的法律费用。
  同时，尼古拉（Nikola）认为，兴登堡（Hindenburg）报告的主要论点-尼古拉（Nikola）是“大规模欺诈”，是不准确的。 Milton确实在尝试建立可行的氢气运输业务，但是能力不强。在他疯狂地夸张尼古拉的能力的同时，他还雇用了经验丰富的工程师来开发其产品。离开之前，弥尔顿与确实知道如何设计和制造卡车的公司建立了交易。
 因此，在尼古拉（Nikola）的新管理层试图超越米尔顿（Milton）的滑稽动作时，他们继承了创始人战略中最有希望的方面。的确，尼古拉在本周发布季度财务业绩时表示，已与意大利卡车制造商依维柯合作完成了尼古拉Tre电池电动卡车的五个原型的组装。尼古拉说，它正在与依维柯（IVECO）合作在德国建立制造工厂，预计于6月开始试生产。尼古拉还在亚利桑那州建立了卡车工厂，希望今年晚些时候开始生产卡车。
     尼古拉说，它将在今年年底之前将卡车交到客户手中。在周四的电话会议上，该公司表示对其电池电动卡车的需求仍然强劲。
 尼古拉还表示，氢电动卡车计划在2023年发布。该公司最近宣布了与亚利桑那州一家电力公司的电力供应协议，该协议将帮助尼古拉为其计划中的氢燃料站网络以低成本生产氢燃料。 
Nikola之所以能够获得最低的电价，是因为在炎热的夏季午后可以暂停制氢。这对于电力公司很有价值，因为他们必须花费大量金钱来确保在需求高峰期有足够的容量。承诺在高峰需求期间消耗零功率的客户增加的成本很少，从而使他们获得了很多收益。尼古拉说，它希望在其他州获得类似的交易。
 简而言之，尼古拉的新管理层确实在努力使尼古拉成为一家可行的卡车公司。他们抛弃了Milton愿景中最荒谬的部分，例如Badger皮卡车，并着眼于更可行的方面。
 但是时钟在滴答作响。尼古拉在2020年第四季度损失了近1.5亿美元，银行中有8.4亿美元。因此，该公司将需要不久筹集更多资金。为此，该公司的领导人需要说服投资者，他们过去曾把Milton当作骗子，现在正在经营一家具有可行业务策略的普通公司。目前尚不清楚他们是否会做到这一点。但是他们正在尝试。
note_en:
Aspiring electric truck maker Nikola has  admitted to the Securities and Exchange Commission that nine statements made by founder Trevor Milton were "inaccurate." Milton was forced to  resign from Nikola in September, shortly after the falsehoods first came to light.
 Between 2016 and 2020, Milton told a series of whoppers about his fledgling truck maker. At a 2016 press event, Milton took to the stage to unveil a prototype of the company's first truck, dubbed the Nikola One. During the event, Milton claimed that the truck "fully functions." In reality, Nikola never got the truck to move under its own power.
  Nikola's most infamous flimflam came in 2018, when the company  released a video of the Nikola One "in motion." In reality, Nikola had towed the inoperative truck to the top of a long, shallow incline and rolled it down, angling the camera so that it looked like it was driving on level ground.
 These and several other misstatements came to light in September after a short-selling firm called Hindenburg Research  published a report declaring Nikola to be a "massive fraud." In response, Nikola commissioned the law firm of Kirkland and Ellis to examine Hindenburg's allegations. While the resulting report hasn't been made public, Nikola has now copped to nine inaccurate statements prior to Milton's September 2020 departure. They include:
 "In July 2016, the Company stated that it owned rights to natural gas wells, and in August 2016 that the wells were used as a backup to solar hydrogen production"
 "In April 2019, Milton stated that solar panels on the roof of the Company’s headquarters produce approximately 18 megawatts of energy per day"
 "In December 2019 and July 2020, Milton stated that the Company 'can produce' over 1,000 kg of hydrogen at the Company’s demo stations and that the Company was 'down below' $3/kg at that time"
    Nikola now admits that these statements "were inaccurate in whole or in part, when made."
 Nikola says Kirkland and Ellis hasn't decided whether the company violated any laws when it made these misstatements. The firm's investigation is ongoing.
 Nikola says it could still face serious legal consequences for these misstatements. Federal prosecutors and the Securities and Exchange Commission both took an interest in the situation last September. Nikola says it can't predict how those investigations will turn out. Nikola has incurred $8 million in legal fees under an indemnification agreement with Trevor Milton.
  At the same time, Nikola argues that the main thesis of the Hindenburg report—that Nikola was a "massive fraud," is not accurate. Milton really was trying to build a viable hydrogen trucking business, however incompetently. While he wildly exaggerated Nikola's capabilities, he also hired experienced engineers to work on its products. Before his departure, Milton forged deals with companies that really do know how to design and build trucks.
 So while Nikola's new management is trying to move past Milton's antics, they've carried forward with the most promising aspects of the founder's strategy. Indeed, when it released its  quarterly financial results this week, Nikola said that it has completed assembly of five prototypes of its Nikola Tre battery electric truck in partnership with IVECO, an Italian truck maker. Nikola says that it's working with IVECO to build a manufacturing facility in Germany, with trial production slated to begin in June. Nikola is also building a truck factory in Arizona that it hopes will begin manufacturing trucks later this year.
     Nikola says it will get trucks into the hands of customers before the end of the year. In a Thursday conference call, the company said that there continues to be strong demand for its battery electric trucks.
 Nikola also said that its hydrogen electric trucks are on track for a planned 2023 release date. The company recently announced an  electricity supply deal with an Arizona electric utility that will help Nikola produce hydrogen fuel at a low cost for its planned network of hydrogen fuel stations.
 Nikola was able to get a rock-bottom price for electricity because hydrogen production can be suspended during hot summer afternoons. That's valuable to electric utilities because they have to spend a lot of money ensuring that they have enough capacity during periods of peak demand. A customer who commits to consuming zero power during periods of peak demand adds very little cost, allowing them to get a great deal. Nikola says it is hoping to secure similar deals in other states.
 In short, Nikola's new management really is trying to turn Nikola into a viable truck company. They've jettisoned the most ridiculous parts of Milton's vision—like the  Badger pickup truck—and are focusing on the more viable aspects.
 But the clock is ticking. Nikola lost almost $150 million in the fourth quarter of 2020 and has $840 million in the bank. So the company is going to need to raise more money before too long. And to do that, the company's leaders need to convince investors that they have put Milton's chicanery in the past and are now running a normal company with a viable business strategy. It's not clear if they're going to make it. But they're trying.

众所周知，目前很难找到最好的显卡，这意味着我们的GPU基准测试体系中的所有产品都售罄，最快的购买方式是在eBay上支付极高的价格。有多极端？我们说去年是购买卡的糟糕时机，但是现在这些价格看起来还不错。现在，最理想的卡的售价几乎是其官方发行价的三倍。
  今年早些时候，迈克尔·德里斯科尔（Michael Driscoll）通过跟踪eBay各种技术组件的黄牛价格来大张旗鼓。他还提供了其工具的源代码，我们已对其进行了修改，并用于继续跟踪此数据。我们将其称为GPU悲伤指数，因为当前的趋势非常令人沮丧。以太坊挖矿仍然足够有利可图，对价格有明显影响，因为没有游戏玩家应该为此支付图形卡官方价格的两倍甚至三倍。
  我们会定期根据最新的价格趋势更新本文。我们选择了过去几代的所有主要GPU，并针对eBay的拍卖进行了查询，对垃圾数据进行了过滤（这意味着，我们会尽最大努力排除卡片图片的列表，或只是盒子）。在黄牛和矿工之间，过去几个月的上升趋势非常明显。查看每张卡的销售数量也很有趣-有些卡显然比其他卡更容易拿到。
 在不进行介绍的情况下，我们来了解一下2021年2月18日至25日的图表（部分数据来自2月25日）。我们已经在39个不同的GPU上收集了数据，并且将根据GPU的代数将这些卡成组地分组。
   从AMD和Nvidia的最新图形卡开始，所有产品的售价都远高于正式发行价。 RTX 3080现在在过去一周中已将eBay的平均价格突破了2,000美元大关，这实际上开始使3090的2889美元中位数价格几乎是合理的。好的，这根本不合理，但是与MSRP的差异114％相比，它的速度快了10％到15％，资金增加了42％。根据eBay价格与MSRP的对比，RTX 3060 Ti最终成为最差的交易。目前：它的价格比RTX 3070高，中位数价格比其建议零售价高三倍。但是3070的性能要好得多，它的价格是其发行价的2.38倍-它也是Ampere GPU中最受欢迎/可用的产品，其销量要比其他三个GPU的总和还多。我们还没有新发布的GeForce RTX 3060 12GB的数据，但是鉴于采矿性能的下降，它有望降落到其官方建议零售价附近。
  AMD的Big Navi GPU讲述了一个类似的故事。 RX 6800的价格是RX 6800 XT官方发布价格的两倍多。 RX 6900 XT是最接近其MSRP的，中位数价格上涨了73％，并且鉴于其实际性能，如果您愿意支付这些价格，则可能会更合乎要求。当然，对于矿工来说，RX 6800仍然是三张卡中的最佳选择，因为这三张卡的哈希率大致相似。
    退后一代，Turing和RDNA1卡主要在2018年和2019年推出，并在2020年初进行了一些更新和添加。某些型号的价格至少接近原始的MSRP，尽管通常我们期望大多数这些卡的价格远低于其原始发行价格。 RTX 20系列GPU的中位数价格仅比发布价格（2080和2080 Ti）高10％，比最低的建议零售价（2060和2060 Super）高80-90％。正如我们在其他地方提到的那样，大多数价格都与以太坊挖矿表现密切相关。 
Nvidia的GTX 16系列Turing GPU也以较高的价格销售，具有6GB或以上VRAM的卡具有较高的价格。与1660卡相比，GTX 1650或1650 Super的价格更低。但是性能也差很多。 1650的价格基本上是发布价格的两倍，而1650 Super的价格是发布价格的两倍。升级到6GB卡，1660 GDDR5型号和1660 Super的价格是MSRP的一倍以上，而1660 Ti的价格实际上比1660 Super的价格低-但是售出的卡数量仅为三分之一。
 AMD的RDNA1 / Navi 1x卡处于类似状态。到目前为止，最受欢迎的显卡是RX 5700 XT，售出280件，中位数价格为927美元。是的，这是发射价格的两倍多。 RX 5700价格更低，但在过去一周仅售出27台，因此祝您好运。同样，RX 5600 XT的价格是原始价格的250％，RX 5500 XT 8GB的价格几乎是其发行价的三倍。
   最后，我们拥有的卡片大多会在三到四年后推出。这些产品的价格远低于其发行价，但在2020年上半年，您通常会以不到其一半的价格找到它们。 GTX 1050和1050 Ti仍然可以以200美元或更低的价格找到，但它们甚至都不是快速的GPU，而且有可能在不到100美元的价格上买到它们。如果您不关心VRAM的限制（您可能应该），那么GTX 1060 3GB的实际价格会比原始发布价格低-可能是由于相对较差的采矿GPU所致。同时，1060 6GB的价格比其正常发布价格高40％，而1070、1070 Ti和1080 Ti的发布价格也刚好高于发布价格。只有GTX 1080的价格低于旧的549美元的价格，这是1080 Ti到货后的调整价格（原本是599美元的卡）。
 与2020年初相比，AMD的Polaris和Vega卡的价格也被严重高估了。还记得RX 570 4GB卡何时才可以以120美元的价格出售吗？好吧，尽管这些天这些矿山并不适合采矿，但它们的中位价格仍为220美元。 8GB卡的价格要高得多，570的价格为413美元，580的价格为449美元-类似的价格，因为它们都具有相似的挖掘性能。 RX 590的价格实际上比580 8GB的价格低一些，这主要是因为周围几乎没有漂浮的RX 590。 RX Vega 56和Vega 64价格也相对接近，目前价格比发布时贵50％，而Radeon VII是AMD最快的挖掘GPU，得益于其1TBps的HBM2带宽，几乎是发布时的三倍。价格。这价格过高，但几乎是Vega 56/64的哈希速度的两倍，而且供应相对有限。
   在过去的一周中，几乎所有图形卡都在继续上升，这主要归功于采矿业的需求-至少，我们无法想象许多PC游戏玩家会排队支付这些价格。有一些例外，但大多数并不是特别值得注意，尤其是只有一周的数据时。 GTX 1650，GTX 1660 Ti，RTX 3060 Ti，RTX 3080，RX 590，RX 5500 XT和RX 6900 XT均呈线性下降趋势，但考虑到它们远未回到正常价格水平。
 不过，隧道尽头可能有一些灯光-微弱和遥远的灯光，但是这些情况很快就会改变。在比特币和以太坊的峰值价格分别超过58,000美元和2,000美元之后，两者在过去几天都下跌了一点。 RTX 3060 Ti的采矿利润目前估计为每天5.67美元，这意味着需要24天的7/24采矿才能以当前汇率收支平衡，而RTX 3080的每日利润估计为8.84美元（扣除电费后） ），则需要232天才能达到收支平衡点（基于eBay过去一周的中位数价格）。
 以太坊的难度也在持续上升-过去一周上升了5％-这意味着用于挖矿的每个GPU所赚的钱都会成比例地减少。真正最有助于GPU价格上涨的是加密货币估值的大幅下降，这在上周发生了一些，但是还有很长的路要走。过去一周，比特币下跌了5.4％，以太坊下跌了14.4％（这是一周前的水平，而不是那个时期的历史高点）。如果两种趋势都继续下去，那么在下个月的某个时间或两个矿工可能会停止对更多硬件的投资。 
同时，Nvidia刚刚宣布赚了100到3亿美元。上一季度对加密货币矿工的估计销售额。坦白说：可能要高得多。实际上，那是在2020年第四季度，所以也许不是，但是2021年第一季度的矿工销售可能至少占所有GPU销量的一半。这只是一个猜测，但是鉴于比特币和以太坊价格的大幅上涨，这一点也不令人惊讶。至少新的RTX 3060 12GB并不是特别吸引人的采矿选择，尽管这并没有阻止它售罄。
